content_type: Male Gift Buyer CTV and Pre-Roll Video Spot
content_type_hook: ECU of a woman's wrist — she is washing dishes, water running, a bangle catching light. Worn, gold from years of contact. Ambient sound only: running water, faint dish clink. No voiceover yet.
why_it_works: Male gift buyers for jewelry face two primary anxieties: choosing wrong and spending appropriately. This spot resolves both in 30 seconds without triggering either anxiety directly. The 'still on her wrist' proof of longevity addresses the 'will she actually wear it?' concern — the most fundamental male gift-buyer objection — through visual demonstration rather than a claim. The opening of a woman washing dishes (not a jewelry ad aesthetic) disarms the viewer by presenting an un-posed, anti-glamour moment where the bangle is incidental, which paradoxically makes it more credible. The 22-year credibility signal functions as quality endorsement for a male viewer who has no prior brand familiarity.

concept.title: Still On Her Wrist

storyboard.visual: Extreme close-up of a woman's wrist — she is washing dishes, hands under running water. A worn bangle catches the light from a kitchen window. The bangle is gold from years of skin contact, clearly not new. Water runs, a dish clinks faintly. She does not look at the camera.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Ambient sound only: running water, faint dish clink. No voiceover. No music. Silence draws viewer into the frame.

storyboard.visual: Man at a kitchen table, morning light. Phone resting in his hand, face-up, not scrolling — he is thinking. A small recognition crosses his face, like he has just thought of something specific.
storyboard.audio_or_text: VO begins (male voice, 38-45, unhurried, not announcer register): 'There's a thing she keeps. Not because it was expensive. Because it meant something when she got it.' No music yet.

storyboard.visual: Man's hand holds a Token of Love Charm Bangle toward the frame — a woman's wrist enters from the left and she takes it from his hand, slides it on. No box, no bow, no velvet. The charm is visible. Natural light.
storyboard.audio_or_text: VO: 'Alex and Ani has been making these bracelets since 2004. Twenty-two years. There is a reason they are still on people's wrists.' Soft acoustic guitar enters — single chord, warm, minimal.

storyboard.visual: Two brief shots in sequence: (1) A younger woman at a coffee shop, bangle incidental on her wrist — she is not looking at it. (2) An older woman in a garden, same style bangle, clearly worn for years. Two ages, two contexts — one unbroken visual argument for longevity.
storyboard.audio_or_text: VO: 'Starting at twenty-eight dollars. Each one comes with the meaning behind it — so she knows exactly why you chose it.' Guitar slight melodic phrase enters.

storyboard.visual: Return to man at the kitchen table. Alex and Ani website visible on his phone screen for exactly one second — he sets the phone face-down. A slight exhale. A private, small smile — the decision is made.
storyboard.audio_or_text: VO (quieter): 'Buy her something that will still be on her wrist in ten years.' Music pulls toward near-silence.

storyboard.visual: Clean close-up of a bangle on a woman's wrist, natural light, no distracting background. The charm is clearly visible. Text overlay appears in clean serif.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Text overlay: 'ALEX AND ANI — Since 2004' / '22 Years.' No voiceover in this beat.

storyboard.visual: Clean end card — white or warm-off-white. 'alexandani.com' centered in clean serif. 'Starting at $28' below in slightly smaller weight.
storyboard.audio_or_text: VO: 'alexandani.com. Starting at twenty-eight dollars.' Music out. URL holds in silence for 2 seconds.

concept.copy: She still has the first meaningful thing someone gave her. Make sure that's from you. Starting at $28.
concept.reference: Inspired by the male gift-buyer creative strategy of David Yurman's holiday CTV spots ('She'll remember this') and Kendra Scott's gift-occasion pre-roll format — adapted by removing luxury aspiration signal and replacing it with longevity proof (worn bangle washing dishes) and rational entry point ($28 price clear and direct). The decision-made exhale device (man setting phone down) draws from Chevy's 'Real People, Not Actors' spot grammar applied to a gift-buyer context.
concept.product_placement_notes: Scene 1: woman's bangle is worn and gold from years of contact — must be a real or prop aged bangle, never new. Scene 3: Token of Love Charm Bangle is the hero product — transferred from man's hand to woman's wrist without box or bow. Scene 4: two women, two ages, same bangle style — visual continuity of the brand across decades. Scene 6: clean wrist shot, charm clearly visible, no distracting background. End card: URL and price only — no product in frame.
concept.talent_direction: Scene 1 woman: real, domestic, not styled. Wrist must look lived-in. Scene 2 man: 38-48, real, not model-handsome, thinking expression must be genuine — direct the recognition moment carefully. Scene 3: man's hand and woman's wrist, no faces required — clean casting brief for hands only. Scene 4: two women, ages 28-35 and 55-65, both wearing bangles as if they have for years — no awareness of camera. Scene 5 man: same as Scene 2 — the exhale and small private smile must be directed as real and internal, not performed for the camera.
